# What “Working” Means for This Vault

An absolute guarantee is not possible for any research archive: external links may move, laws and policies may change, and new reporting may supersede older sources. The correct standard is **verifiability**.

This vault is designed to work in four concrete ways:

## 1. It opens and extracts cleanly

The zip should extract into one top-level folder. The `14_QA_VALIDATION` folder contains a manifest and checksum files so you can verify the contents.

## 2. It is navigable

The vault is organized into start-here notes, frameworks, domain maps, tactic cards, case studies, detection methods, law/policy notes, paper/slide outputs, source indexes, appendices, deep dives, matrices, study guides, and QA files.

## 3. It supports a defensible paper

Each domain is tied to a signal layer and an evidence class. The evidence ladder separates regulator/platform sources, academic sources, reporting, industry artifacts, and anecdotal/community material.

## 4. It keeps the ethics boundary clear

The vault explains how manufactured traction is studied and detected. It avoids framing deceptive activity as a recipe to run.

## Practical QA checklist

- [ ] The zip extracts without error.
- [ ] `README.md` is visible in the root folder.
- [ ] `14_QA_VALIDATION/QA_REPORT.md` exists.
- [ ] `14_QA_VALIDATION/file_manifest.csv` exists.
- [ ] `14_QA_VALIDATION/SHA256SUMS.txt` exists.
- [ ] `15_RESEARCH_PATTERN_ATLAS/00_Overview.md` exists.
- [ ] `03_TACTIC_CARDS` contains tactic files grouped by signal layer.
- [ ] All files are `.md`, `.csv`, `.json`, `.txt`, or `.py`.
- [ ] No file is intentionally empty.

## When to update

Update the source index when a regulator publishes new guidance, a platform revises a policy, or a case study develops further. Live areas to re-check: FTC review enforcement, ACCC influencer cases, platform fake-engagement policies, AI-generated music policies, and ad-scam enforcement.
